sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Big goals are hard to achieve all at once; breaking them into small steps is wiser. We present Trust Region Policy Distillation (TOP-D), which transforms the notoriously unstable, high-variance On-Policy Distillation (OPD) into a stable training paradigm by dynamically constructing a proximal teacher. Theoretically, we establish a rigorous framework demonstrating that TOP-D inherently controls gradient variance. By providing a formal global convergence analysis alongside a monotonic improvement bound, we mathematically formalize the reliability and stability of the overall training dynamics. Empirically, TOP-D dramatically enhances training stability, sample efficiency, and final performance on mathematical reasoning tasks. More importantly, TOP-D introduces zero additional computational overhead, positioning itself as a promising alternative to the well-established OPD paradigm.

[ 1 1 5 7 4 0 . 7 0 6 2 : r Figure 1: While standard OPD forces direct teacher supervision and suffers from unstable optimization, TOP-D ensures stable training by dynamically constructing proximal teacher at every iteration.

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Big goals are hard to achieve all at once; breaking them into small steps is wiser. We present Trust Region Policy Distillation (TOP-D), which transforms the notoriously unstable, high-variance On-Policy Distillation (OPD) into stable training paradigm by dynamically constructing proximal teacher. Theoretically, we establish rigorous framework demonstrating that TOP-D inherently controls gradient variance. By providing formal global convergence analysis alongside monotonic improvement bound, we mathematically formalize the reliability and stability of the overall training dynamics. Empirically, TOP-D dramatically enhances training stability, sample efficiency, and final performance on mathematical reasoning tasks. More importantly, TOP-D introduces zero additional computational overhead, positioning itself as promising alternative to the well-established OPD paradigm. Figure 2: The avg@32 accuracy on several AIME benchmarks for RLVR applied to the Qwen3-8BBase model, and for OPD and TOP-D using Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct-2507 as the teacher model. sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: On-Policy Distillation (OPD) [10] has rapidly emerged as the prevailing paradigm for post-training Large Language Models (LLMs) [18, 16, 21, 19, 1], in which the student learns from token-level signals provided by an external teacher. Mathematically, this is equivalent to reinforcement learning where the immediate reward is defined by the logarithmic probability ratio between the teacher and student policies. Consequently, the on-policy nature of OPD prevents the catastrophic forgetting of Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) [14], while its dense rewards overcome the sample inefficiency and instability of Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) (as summarized in Table 1). Despite its theoretical appeal, standard OPD suffers from severe optimization fragility in practice. The fundamental bottleneck of OPD lies in the inherent capacity gap between the target teacher and the student [5]. Because the standard distillation objective is driven by the logarithmic probability ratio between the two policies, the optimization dynamics are highly vulnerable to teacher-student disagreements. Recent studies have devoted considerable engineering efforts to mitigate this instability through various techniques, including teacher-student mixed sampling [3], reward clipping [7], top-p sampling [2], off-policy cold starts [9], and scaling to full-vocabulary supervision [1]. However, these mitigation strategies remain largely empirical heuristics that fail to capture the underlying mechanism of the instability: the unbounded logarithmic probability difference between the teacher and student. Furthermore, these empirical methods inherently lack theoretical guarantees for reliable optimization. In this paper, we present Trust Region Policy Distillation (TOP-D) to systematically resolve the inherent optimization instability of standard OPD. Specifically, to overcome the unbounded logarithmic signals, we dynamically construct an external proximal teacher (as illustrated in Figure 1 and Section 3.1) by interpolating the probability distributions of the target teacher and the current student. This mathematical construction transforms the unbounded distillation reward into smooth, strictly lower-bounded signal, analytically preventing variance explosion. Building upon this stabilized objective, we further incorporate internal trust region iterations (described in Section 3.2) to better approximate the current proximal teacher, while simultaneously improving overall sample efficiency. Crucially, while the proximal teacher is constructed conceptually, the practical objective mathematically reduces to simple algebraic transformation of the standard token-level OPD reward. As result, TOP-D introduces zero additional computational overhead, serving as plug-and-play module that is natively compatible with both state-of-the-art distributed training infrastructures [15, 22] and high-throughput inference engines [8, 23]. Beyond this empirical elegance, our proposed framework establishes rigorous theoretical closed-loop: while the external proximal teacher strictly bounds the gradient variance (described in Section 4.1), the internal trust region iterations guarantee monotonic policy improvement (described in Section 4.3), systematically minimizing the single-step optimization error to tightly close the overall asymptotic convergence gap (described in Section 4.2). In summary, the main contributions of this paper are as follows: We propose TOP-D, plug-and-play distillation paradigm that smooths the unbounded reward signal and safely breaks the strict on-policy data-reuse barrier of standard OPD. We establish rigorous theoretical framework where the proximal teacher inherently bounds gradient variance to stabilize training, while our convergence analysis and monotonic improvement bound form closed-loop to systematically minimize the asymptotic error. We empirically demonstrate that TOP-D dramatically improves reasoning capabilities on mathematical reasoning tasks, delivering 25.84% absolute improvement in avg@32 accuracy over standard OPD on AIME24 and consistently dominating RLVR and OPD baselines.

sections.title: 2 Preliminaries
sections.content: 2.1 On-Policy Distillation Suppose we have an autoregressive language model πθ, where θ Θ denotes the parameters. Given prompt Dx, the model generates response = (y0, y1, . . . , yT )2 autoregressively, where = is the response length. Formally, log πθ(y x) = (cid:88) t=1 log πθ(yt x, y<t), (1) where y<t denotes (y0, y1, . . . , yt1). In the context of OPD, we aim to minimize the following reverse KL divergence: θ = arg min L(θ) = arg min θ ExDx [DKL(πθ( x), π( x))] (cid:21) (cid:20) = arg max θ ExDx,yπθ(x) log π(y x) πθ(y x) = arg max θ J(θ), (2) θ where π is typically stronger model. We are now ready to present the standard policy gradient form of the objective (2): θJ(θ) = ExDx,yπθ(x) (cid:88) θ log πθ(yt x, y<t) (cid:88) log ρk , (3) t=1 k=t where ρk = π(ykx,y<k) πθ(ykx,y<k) . In other words, we can treat log ρk as the immediate reward for the k-th token, i.e., rk = log ρk. However, the cumulative reward (cid:80)y k=t rk introduces an inherent length bias as well as high variance. In practice, common approach is to use the immediate reward rk directly. 2.2 Reinforcement Learning Formulation To establish rigorous theoretical foundation, we cast autoregressive language modeling as deterministic Markov Decision Process (MDP) defined by the tuple (S, A, P, r, µ0). State space and action space A. State st = (x, y<t) represents the prompt and prefix. The action space = includes the vocabulary and the EOS token. To rigorously handle variable-length sequences, we augment with permanent absorbing state s. Dynamics and initial distribution µ0. The initial state distribution µ0(s) follows the prompt distribution Dx and the special initial token y0. Transitions are deterministic: selecting = EOS appends to the prefix, while selecting EOS definitively transitions to s. Reward function r. The immediate reward is the distillation signal: r(s, a) = log π(as) π(as) . To prevent infinite accumulation after termination3, we set r(s, a) 0 for any A. Let π : (A) be stochastic policy. Operating in the undiscounted setting (γ = 1), the action value function Qπ(s, a) is defined as Qπ(s, a) = Eπ [(cid:80) k=0 r(st+k, at+k) st = s, at = a]. The state value function π(s) is strictly obtained by marginalizing Qπ over the policy: π(s) = Eaπ(s) [Qπ(s, a)]. Consequently, we define the advantage function, which forms the basis for policy improvement bounds, as Aπ(s, a) = Qπ(s, a) π(s). To decouple sequential dependencies, we introduce the unnormalized state distribution dπ(s) = (cid:80) P(st = π). The true objective of t=1 policy π is the expected cumulative reward over the infinite horizon: (cid:35) η(π) = ExDx,yπ(x) r(st, at) . (4) (cid:34) (cid:88) t=1 While this abstract MDP formalization is adopted for the theoretical proofs in Section 4.3, the remainder of the paper defaults to the notation established in Section 2.1. 2Here, y0 denotes the special initial token and is not counted toward the response length. 3Assume that π(EOS s) = 1.

sections.title: 3 Methodology
sections.content: This section details the design of our core algorithm, Trust Region Policy Distillation (TOP-D). We begin in Section 3.1 by constructing proximal teacher to stabilize training. Subsequently, in Section 3.2, the current student approximates this proximal teacher by performing trust region iterations. Finally, Section 3.3 outlines the practical implementation of the TOP-D algorithm. 3.1 External Proximal Teacher The fundamental challenge in standard On-Policy Distillation (OPD) arises from the inherent capacity gap between the student policy πθ and the target teacher policy π. As defined in Equation (3), the optimization dynamics heavily rely on the probability ratio ρk = π(ykx,y<k) πθ(ykx,y<k) . In extreme cases where the teacher assigns near-zero probability to generated token (π 0), the immediate reward rk = log ρk diverges to negative infinity. This unbounded penalty destabilizes the policy gradient, making the optimization process extremely fragile. To address this instability, we draw inspiration from trust region methods in reinforcement learning [11, 12, 17], which restrict policy updates from taking destructively large steps. Rather than forcing the student to perfectly imitate rigid teacher, we dynamically construct an intermediate, localized target distribution. We term this surrogate target the proximal teacher π. Formally, π(yk x, y<k) = απ(yk x, y<k)+(1α)πθ(yk x, y<k), where α (0, 1) is the coefficient that controls the interpolation strength. In fact, the proximal teacher inherently smooths the token-level reward rk. Specifically, π(yk x, y<k) πθ(yk x, y<k) (cid:18) Figure 3: Reward curves of rk and rk. α = log rk = log π(yk x, y<k) πθ(yk x, y<k) When α = 1, it reduces to the original OPD reward rk. Thanks to mathematics, we do not need to explicitly construct proximal teacher to obtain the new reward rk. Figure 3 visualizes the OPD reward rk and our proposed reward rk with respect to the probability ratio ρk. We can see that rk is strictly bounded from below4 as ρk 0. Moreover, we explicitly justify our choice of interpolating in the probability space instead of the log-probability space to construct the proximal teacher, since setting log π(yk x, y<k) = α log π(yk x, y<k) + (1 α) log πθ(yk x, y<k) yields = log (αρk + 1 α) . + 1 α (5) (cid:19) which is merely naive scaling of the original OPD reward. 3.2 Internal Trust Region Iterations rk = αrk, (6) With the proximal teacher established, the student policy is optimized to maximize the expected cumulative reward Rt = (cid:80)y k=t rk. While standard OPD relies on continuous online sampling, it fundamentally operates in strictly on-policy manner. In the naive formulation, once the student policy is updated, the previously generated trajectories are immediately discarded. This results in notoriously low sample efficiency, lacking the capacity to reuse data. Inspired by modern trust region algorithms [11, 12, 17], we analogously decouple the behavior policy πθold and the target policy πθ to inherit their high sample efficiency. Specifically, J(θ) = xDx {yi}G i=1πθold (x) 1 (cid:80)G i=1yi (cid:88) yi (cid:88) i= t=1 (cid:110) min (cid:104) ˆAi pi t, clip (cid:0)pi t, 1 ϵ, 1 + ϵ(cid:1) ˆAi (cid:105)(cid:111) . (7) Here, we follow the group-based reinforcement learning settings [13, 4], where the superscript tx,yi <t) indexes different model responses. pi <t) is the importance sampling ratio. We adopt tx,yi = πθ(yi πθold (yi 4log(αx + 1 α) log(1 α) when (0, +) and α (0, 1). 4 Figure 4: In contrast to sequence-level advantage normalization, we perform token-level normalization across the responses generated for given prompt to better leverage the dense reward signal. Algorithm 1 Trust Region Policy Distillation (TOP-D) Require: Student πθ, teacher π, dataset Dx, interpolation α, group size G, internal epochs 1: while not converged do Initialize πθold πθ 2: Sample batch of prompts Dx 3: for each prompt do 4: 5: 6: 7: 8: 9: 10: 11: Perform rollout to sample responses {yi}G α π(yi Compute TOP-D rewards ri πθold (yi + 1 yik Ri kµ σ Compute token-level returns Ri Compute token-level advantages ˆAi ri log (cid:16) i=1 πθold ( x) kx,yi <k) <k) + 1 α kx,yi j=k+1 ri (cid:80)yi (cid:17) end for for epoch = 1 to do (cid:80)G J(θ) 1 (cid:80)G Update θ by maximizing J(θ) using an optimizer i=1yi ˆAi pi min (cid:80)yi t= t, clip (cid:0)pi i=1 (cid:110) (cid:104) t, 1 ϵ, 1 + ϵ(cid:1) ˆAi (cid:105)(cid:111) 12: end for 13: 14: end while more fine-grained token-level normalized advantage ˆAi to better exploit dense reward signals, as illustrated in Figure 4. For each prompt, the advantage relies on token-level reward Rt, which combines the immediate reward and length-normalized future return [3], i.e., Ri = ri + 1 yi yi (cid:88) ri j. j=k+1 (8) We empirically observe that this prevents the model from generating overly short or excessively long responses. The mean µ and standard deviation σ within each group are calculated via µ = mean{ Ri k} and σ = std{ Ri k}, where = 1, . . . , and = 1, . . . , yi. 3.3 Trust Region Policy Distillation In this section, we synthesize the concepts of the external proximal teacher in Section 3.1 and the internal trust region iterations in Section 3.2 to formulate our complete algorithmic framework, Trust Region Policy Distillation (TOP-D), summarized in Algorithm 1. Standard OPD suffers from two fundamental bottlenecks: optimization fragility and low sample efficiency. TOP-D systematically resolves both through unified design. First, the external proximal teacher transforms the unbounded OPD reward into smooth, lower-bounded reward rk, intrinsically stabilizing the optimization process. Built upon this principle, the internal trust region iterations decouple the behavior and target policies, breaking the strict on-policy data-reuse barrier. Crucially, these mechanisms are not merely empirical heuristics but share deep theoretical synergy. We rigorously formalize this framework and establish its mathematical guarantees in the Section 4.

sections.title: 4 Theoretical Analysis
sections.content: In this section, we establish rigorous theoretical foundation for TOP-D. We begin in Section 4.1 by proving that the proximal teacher inherently bounds gradient variance to stabilize optimization. Building upon this, we derive global convergence bound in Section 4.2 by abstracting the learning dynamics. Finally, to minimize the local error bottleneck identified in this convergence analysis, we establish the monotonic improvement guarantee for our internal trust region iterations in Section 4.3. 4.1 Bounded Variance To rigorously analyze the gradient stability, we restrict our discussion to the autoregressive generation environment over finite vocabulary V. In policy gradient methods, the variance of the gradient is typically dominated by its second moment, i.e., Var [g] (cid:2)g2(cid:3). Thus, our theoretical analysis will primarily focus on establishing an absolute upper bound for the gradients second moment. Assumption 4.1. We assume that the gradient of the student policys log-probability (the score function) is uniformly bounded. That is, there exists constant > 0, such that for any prompt Dx, generated response π( x), and k-th token yk, we have θ log πθ(yk x, y<k) M. (9) Let gk = θ log πθ(yk x, y<k) rk denote the token-level policy gradient estimator for standard OPD, and gk = θ log πθ(yk x, y<k) rk denote the gradient estimator for our proposed TOP-D. Theorem 4.2. Under Assumption 4.1, for any interpolation coefficient α (0, 1), the variance of the TOP-D gradient estimator gk does not diverge across the entire probability space. Instead, it is strictly bounded by uniform absolute limit: Var(gk) 2V max (cid:110) (log(1 α))2 , α (cid:111) , (10) where is universal mathematical constant, and denotes the size of the vocabulary V. Proof. See Appendix C.1. Remark 4.3. TOP-D employs an asymmetric operator to stabilize training. For heavy penalizations, it introduces hard safety bound, (log(1 α))2, preventing catastrophic divergence; for high rewards, it applies linear dampening scaled by α. This effectively designates α as direct variance controller. As α 1, TOP-D recovers standard OPD, with its upper bound diverging ((log(1 α))2 ) to reflect OPDs unbounded variance. Conversely, as α 0+, the variance vanishes. 4.2 Convergence Analysis To rigorously analyze the convergence properties of TOP-D, we abstract the construction of the proximal teacher as an iterative operator mapping over the policy space. Let Π denote the space of all valid policies. To quantify the divergence within this space, we introduce the expected L1 distance between any two policies πi, πj as d(πi, πj) = ExDx [πi( x) πj( x)1]. Next, we formalize the iterative process through the proximal teacher operator : Π Π, given by (π) = απ + (1 α)π, (11) where α (0, 1) is the interpolation coefficient, and π is the target teacher. Building upon this metric space, we are now ready to establish the following convergence analysis: Theorem 4.4. For any initial student policy π0 Π, consider the iterative update process πk+1 = 5, where ϵk accounts for the practical optimization error (e.g., function approximation (πk) + ϵk and optimization noise) at the k-th global step, where 0. Then, the following bound holds: d(πk+1, π) (1 α)k+1d(π0, π) + (cid:88) i=0 (1 α)kiϵi1. (12) Furthermore, assuming the optimization process eventually stabilizes such that the asymptotic error is bounded by lim supkϵk1 ϵ, we have lim sup d(πk+1, π) ϵ α . (13) 5With slight abuse of notation, let ϵk1 = d(πk+1, π k), where π = (πk) is the proximal teacher at the k-th step. 6 Proof. See Appendix C.2. Remark 4.5. The bound presented in Theorem 4.4 not only reveals an inherent error forgetting mechanism that isolates early high-variance updates, but also highlights fundamental theoretical trade-off : the asymptotic convergence gap is strictly bounded by ϵ/α. This implies that to break through the precision ceiling without compromising stability (i.e., keeping α small), the only viable path is to strictly minimize the single-step optimization error ϵk1. This theoretical bottleneck motivates the necessity of introducing internal trust region iterations in the subsequent section. 4.3 Monotonic Improvement As revealed in Theorem 4.4, the fundamental theoretical bottleneck of the proximal teacher framework lies in the asymptotic convergence gap bounded by ϵ/α. To tightly close this gap without compromising optimization stability (i.e., avoiding excessively large α), it is imperative to rigorously minimize the single-step optimization error ϵk1. TOP-D achieves this via internal trust region iterations. In this section, we establish the theoretical insight of this mechanism by proving monotonic policy improvement. For mathematical rigor, we follow the notation established in Section 2.2. Assumption 4.6. Given the fixed context window constraints inherent to autoregressive language models, we assume that any valid policy π Π generates the EOS token within finite maximum horizon Tmax. Consequently, the response length strictly satisfies Tmax, so (cid:80) dπ(s) < . s=s In our undiscounted setting (γ = 1), the unnormalized measure dπ(s) diverges because the permanent absorbing state dominates as . To construct valid probability distribution over the meaningful transient states, we explicitly exclude and normalize dπ(s). Definition 4.7. We formally define the expected response length of policy π as ℓπ = ExDx,yπ(x) [y]. Excluding s, the sum of the state distribution precisely evaluates to this length. Thus, we define the normalized state visitation measure dπ Remark 4.8. This normalized measure enables an equivalent reformulation of the Performance Difference Lemma [6], strictly decoupling the sequence length from the single-step generation quality. We can thus rewrite the original objective (4) as η(π) = ℓπ Esdπ now ready to present the main theoretical result of this section: Theorem 4.9. Given Assumption 4.6 and Definition 4.7, for any two policies, π and π, the following bound holds: norm(),aπ(s) [r(s, a)], and we are norm(s) = dπ(s) , = s. ℓπ η(π) ζπ(π) 2ξTmaxℓπEsdπ norm() [DTV(π( s), π( s))] , where ζπ(π) = η(π) + ℓπEsdπ norm(),aπ(s) [Aπ(s, a)], and ξ = maxsEaπ(s) [Aπ(s, a)]. (14) Proof. See Appendix C.3. Remark 4.10. Let Mπ(π) denote the lower bound established in Theorem 4.9. By definition, η(π) = Mπ(π). Therefore, any policy update that improves the lower bound guarantees monotonic improvement on the true objective, i.e., η(π) η(π) Mπ(π) Mπ(π). As result, optimizing Mπ(π) ensures that the original reverse KL divergence objective monotonically improves during the internal updates. Let the superscript (n) denote the n-th step of the internal trust region iterations at the k-th global step, and let π = (πk) be the fixed proximal teacher. By iteratively optimizing Mπ(π), we obtain monotonically decreasing sequence of KL divergences: D(1) KL ExDx ExDx (cid:104) D(0) KL D(n) KL (15) (cid:104) (cid:105) (cid:105) (cid:104) (cid:105) , where D(j) k( x)) denotes the reverse KL divergence objective at the KL j-th internal iteration. If the internal trust region optimization proceeds until the final expected divergence satisfies ExDx 2 δ2 where δ > 0, then by applying Pinskers inequality ( x), π (cid:105) (cid:104) D(n) KL ϵk1 = d(πk+1, π k) = d(π(n) , π k) ExDx 2D(n) KL (cid:20)(cid:113) (cid:21) (cid:114) 2ExDx (cid:105) (cid:104) D(n) KL δ alongside ExDx DKL(π(j) Jensens inequality, we rigorously guarantee that the optimization error satisfies ϵk1 δ. This perfectly closes the overall theoretical loop: the internal trust region iterations mathematically guarantee the continuous minimization of the single-step optimization error ϵk, thereby enabling the algorithm to tightly close the asymptotic convergence gap ϵ/α defined in Theorem 4.4.

sections.title: 5 Experiments
sections.content: In this section, we empirically validate the effectiveness of TOP-D. Specifically, Section 5.1 details our validation datasets, model configurations, baseline methods, and implementation hyperparameters. Section 5.2 presents comprehensive comparison of TOP-D against existing post-training baselines. Finally, Section 5.3 provides an in-depth analysis of the core components of our method, including the external proximal teacher and the internal trust region iterations, as well as the sensitivity analysis. 5.1 Experimental Setup Datasets and benchmarks. We conduct our training phase using the DAPO-Math-17k [20] dataset, high-quality mathematical reasoning corpus. For validation, we benchmark the post-trained models on widely recognized and challenging mathematical reasoning competitions like AIME and AMC. Models and baselines. To comprehensively investigate the scalability of TOP-D and its robustness across different teacher-student capacity gaps, we employ two distinct base models as students: Qwen3-1.7B-Base [18] and Qwen3-8B-Base [18]. For the target teacher policies, we utilize Qwen314B [18] and the exceptionally capable Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct-2507 [18]. We compare TOP-D against several post-training paradigms including GRPO [13], DAPO [20], and standard OPD [10]. Implementation details. During the rollout phase, the student model generates 8 responses for each prompt. To execute the trust region iterations, we follow the same off-policy setting as RLVR by dividing the same batch of data into multiple mini-batches, where the global batch size is set to 512 prompts (4096 samples) and the mini-batch size is set to 32 prompts (256 samples). The core interpolation coefficient α for TOP-D is set to 0.1 or 0.2. During training, we apply sampling temperature of 1.0 and top-p of 1.0. For performance validation, we use temperature of 1.0 and top-p of 0.7. Comprehensive details regarding other hyperparameters are provided in Appendix B. Student Model Paradigm Method Teacher Model Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct-2507 avg@32 avg@ AIME24 AIME25 AIME26 AMC23 MATH-500 Olympiad Base Qwen3-8B-Base RLVR - GRPO DAPO 9.38 30.10 32. 8.02 22.08 27.81 6.15 21.67 32.29 53.13 56.33 65. 75.23 76.83 81.65 40.08 44.92 44.23 Distillation OPD TOP-D (Ours) 24.58 50.42 (+25.84) 23.33 34.06 (+10.73) 25.42 44.06 (+18.64) 76.88 88.13 (+11.25) 87.98 91.23 (+3.25) 59.29 64.67 (+5.38) Table 2: Performance comparison of the Qwen3-8B-Base across various mathematical benchmarks. Student Model Paradigm Method Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct-2507 Qwen3-14B Teacher Model avg@32 AIME24 AIME25 AIME26 AIME AIME25 AIME26 Base Qwen3-1.7B-Base RLVR - GRPO DAPO 3.44 10.52 12.29 1.98 7.19 11.56 1. 4.69 8.54 3.44 10.52 12.29 1.98 7.19 11.56 1. 4.69 8.54 Distillation OPD TOP-D (Ours) 8.96 20.31 (+11.35) 7.50 17.71 (+10.21) 5.94 13.75 (+7.81) 7.81 12.81 (+5.00) 8.33 14.69 (+6.36) 5.63 9.69 (+4.06) Table 3: Performance comparison of the Qwen3-1.7B-Base across AIME benchmarks. 5.2 Main Results Table 2 and Table 3 summarize the validation results of TOP-D compared to other baselines. TOP-D consistently outperforms the evaluated baselines across all mathematical benchmarks and student model scales. The most striking improvement is observed when compared to standard OPD. For instance, using the Qwen3-8B-Base student supervised by the Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct-2507 teacher, TOP-D achieves an avg@32 accuracy of 50.42% on AIME24, delivering massive 25.84% absolute 8 Figure 5: Learning curves for the ablation study on the Qwen3-1.7B-Base. We isolate the effects of the external proximal teacher (α = 1.0) and the internal trust region iterations (w/o off-policy). improvement over standard OPD (24.58%). This immense performance gain definitively proves that bounding the variance of the distillation reward effectively stabilizes the training process and subsequently unlocks the students reasoning potential. Furthermore, TOP-D firmly dominates the RLVR baselines, exceeding the highly competitive DAPO by 17.5% absolute accuracy on the same benchmark, demonstrating the superiority of stabilized TOP-D over the traditional RLVR baselines. When scaling down to the smaller Qwen3-1.7B-Base student, standard OPD suffers from severe performance degradation, noticeably falling behind the RLVR baselines. This fragility highlights the vulnerability of the unbounded logarithmic probability ratio when the capacity gap between the teacher and the student is severe. In stark contrast, TOP-D consistently maintains its substantial advantage. For the 1.7B student, TOP-D pushes the AIME24 accuracy past the 20% threshold and consistently surpasses standard OPD by over 10 percentage points on both AIME24 and AIME25. 5.3 Ablation Study To deeply understand the mechanisms driving TOP-Ds success, we isolate the impact of its core structural designs. For this analysis, we utilize the Qwen3-1.7B-Base student model supervised by the Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct-2507 teacher. Figure 5 illustrates the learning curves of TOP-D compared to its ablated variants and the standard OPD baseline throughout the entire model training process. By setting the interpolation coefficient to α = 1.0, we remove the external proximal teacher and revert the objective to the unbounded standard OPD reward. As shown in the training curves, this ablation causes highly unstable training dynamics and significant degradation in overall performance. On the other hand, by disabling the internal trust region iterations (denoted as w/o off-policy), we force the optimization into strictly on-policy regime. This leads to drastic drop in sample efficiency, requiring significantly more training steps to converge and climb the performance curve. These empirical results clearly demonstrate that both the external proximal teacher and the internal trust region iterations are indispensable components for achieving optimization stability and rapid performance improvement. Furthermore, we evaluate the sensitivity of TOP-D to the interpolation coefficient by varying α {0.1, 0.2, 0.3}. We empirically observe that the training stability, convergence speed, and final performance exhibit marginal differences across this interval. This demonstrates that TOP-D is highly robust to the choice of α, functioning as reliable solution without the need for exhaustive and computationally expensive hyperparameter tuning efforts.

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: In this paper, we introduce Trust Region Policy Distillation (TOP-D), principled post-training paradigm resolving the optimization instability and sample inefficiency of standard On-Policy Distillation (OPD). Theoretically, TOP-D bounds gradient variance via an external proximal teacher and guarantees monotonic improvement with safe off-policy data reuse through internal trust region iterations. Empirically, it achieves massive performance gains on mathematical reasoning benchmarks, decisively outperforming standard OPD and competitive RLVR baselines across multiple model scales. By achieving these advancements without any additional computational overhead, TOP-D establishes highly reliable, sample-efficient, and robust paradigm for aligning foundation models.

sections.title: References

sections.title: Computational Resources
sections.content: All primary experiments, including the training of the TOP-D and the reproduction of baseline methods, were conducted on high-performance computing cluster equipped with NVIDIA H200 GPUs. To accelerate the research iteration cycle, our standard distributed training setup utilized 4 compute nodes, with each node containing 8 H200 GPUs (amounting to 32 GPUs in total). However, we emphasize that our proposed method is highly resource-efficient and accessible. The entire TOP-D training pipeline and its reported results can be fully reproduced on single standard 8-GPU node.

sections.title: Limitations
sections.content: Although TOP-D establishes highly reliable and sample-efficient paradigm for policy distillation, yielding massive improvements over existing baselines, our current validation is naturally bounded by computational budgets and time constraints. Specifically, our primary validations focus on student models up to the 8B parameter scale. Due to resource limitations, we have not yet investigated the scaling behavior of TOP-D on massive-scale foundation models (e.g., scaling the student model beyond 30B parameters). However, given the theoretically grounded variance-reduction properties of our proximal teacher and the guaranteed monotonic improvement from our trust region iterations, we are optimistic that the robust performance of TOP-D will seamlessly transfer to larger-scale regimes. Furthermore, our current empirical evaluations are constrained to relatively short optimization window of only approximately 200 to 400 update steps (except for the RLVR baselines). Crucially, we have not observed any empirical signs of performance saturation within this timeframe. This strongly suggests that the impressive results reported in our main text are far from their upper bounds, leaving substantial room for further improvements with continued training. We leave the exploration of such extreme-scale distillation and extended training horizons as exciting avenues for future work.
